audits.scope: dYdX Solo Margin / Perpetuals smart contracts (v2/v3 Ethereum)
audits.summary: Audit of core margin and perpetuals contracts found one high-severity issue (since fixed) related to liquidation logic edge cases.

audits.scope: dYdX Solo Margin Protocol smart contracts
audits.summary: OpenZeppelin audited the Solo Margin contracts and found no critical issues; medium findings related to access control and arithmetic edge cases were addressed.

audits.scope: dYdX Perpetuals v3 (StarkEx integration, smart contracts)
audits.summary: Trail of Bits reviewed the StarkEx-integrated perpetuals contracts and identified one high-severity issue around operator key management; all findings were remediated.

audits.scope: dYdX Governance / DYDX token contracts (formal verification)
audits.summary: Certora performed formal verification on the DYDX governance and token contracts, confirming core invariants hold with one medium finding in delegation logic.

audits.scope: dYdX v4 Chain (Cosmos SDK appchain — x/clob, x/perpetuals, x/prices modules)
audits.summary: Informal Systems audited the dYdX v4 Cosmos appchain modules pre-launch and found two high-severity issues in the CLOB matching engine and oracle price validation, both remediated before mainnet.

audits.scope: dYdX v4 Chain smart contracts and off-chain order book components
audits.summary: Veridise reviewed v4 chain components and found one high-severity issue in order validation logic that was fixed prior to mainnet launch.

audits.scope: dYdX v4 risk parameters, liquidation engine, and oracle configuration
audits.summary: Chaos Labs conducted an economic/risk audit of v4 liquidation and margin parameters, recommending tighter oracle deviation thresholds (implemented).

exploit_history.description: dYdX v3 experienced abnormal liquidations during the FTX contagion period; a large position in SUSHI was manipulated via low-liquidity oracle pricing, causing the insurance fund to lose approximately $9M. Not a smart contract exploit — an economic/oracle manipulation attack on a thin market.

Shortly after dYdX v4 mainnet launch, a targeted attack drained the v4 insurance fund of approximately $9M by manipulating YFI perpetual market prices. Attackers built large positions then pumped YFI price to trigger favorable liquidations. dYdX acknowledged the attack was an economic exploit, not a code vulnerability.
Follow-on attack on dYdX v4 targeting LINK and MKR perpetual markets using the same oracle manipulation / thin liquidity strategy, draining additional insurance fund reserves estimated at ~$5M.

dependencies.provider: Skip Protocol / Slinky (formerly Band Protocol sidecar) — dYdX v4 uses validator-reported oracle prices via Slinky (Skip's oracle sidecar) aggregated on-chain
dependencies.notes: Oracle prices are aggregated from multiple validators running Slinky sidecars pulling from CEX APIs (Binance, Coinbase, etc.). Thin-market manipulation was exploited in Nov 2023 YFI/LINK/MKR incidents. dYdX has since tightened oracle deviation parameters and added market caps on open interest for new listings.

dependencies.provider: Noble (USDC native issuance via IBC) + Axelar (cross-chain USDC bridging)
dependencies.notes: dYdX v4 uses Noble for native USDC via IBC and Axelar for bridging from Ethereum. Axelar has its own validator set and smart contract risk. A bridge exploit on Axelar or Noble could affect USDC deposits into dYdX Chain.

dependencies.provider: dYdX Chain validators (Cosmos Tendermint BFT — decentralized validator set)
dependencies.notes: Unlike centralized sequencers (Optimism/Arbitrum), dYdX v4 uses a decentralized Tendermint validator set (~60 active validators). MEV and front-running risk exists but is mitigated by in-protocol MEV revenue sharing. No single sequencer failure point.

dependencies.provider: Ethereum (for DYDX token, governance, and legacy v3 StarkEx contracts)
dependencies.notes: The DYDX ERC-20 token and legacy v3 perpetuals remain on Ethereum. The wethDYDX wrapper contract bridges governance tokens to dYdX Chain. Ethereum L1 risk is systemic/low. StarkEx v3 is being wound down.

rationale: dYdX has a mature audit history with 7+ audits from reputable firms covering both v3 (StarkEx) and v4 (Cosmos appchain) codebases, and no smart contract code exploits to date — all losses (~$23M total) stemmed from economic/oracle manipulation attacks on thin perpetual markets in Nov 2022 and Nov 2023, which have since been partially mitigated via tighter risk parameters and OI caps. The v4 appchain architecture eliminates centralized sequencer risk but introduces Cosmos SDK and IBC dependency risks, and the oracle model (validator-aggregated CEX prices) remains the primary attack surface. The $2M Immunefi bug bounty and ongoing audit cadence reflect a serious security culture, though the repeated insurance fund drains in 2023 indicate risk parameter governance needs continued vigilance.
